text: Narrative 1 - 'The stick shaker activated during a visual approach to XXR ZZZ; with flaps 25 and speed brakes extended. I retracted the speed brake as soon as the shaker activated; which deactivated the shaker.The controller held us at 6000 ft. to allow crossing traffic at 5500 ft. below the localizer. At this point; we were 6000 ft. flaps 5; 170 kts.; (flaps 5 maneuvering speed). The controller advised; 'Traffic no factor'; instructed a descent; I called the field in sight and he cleared us for the visual XXR. Crossing traffic left us high on profile; but we had enough distance; I determined we could continue the visual and be stable at 1000 ft.; which we were.In an effort to rejoin the profile; the First Officer (FO) extended the speed brakes; called for flaps 15; then turned off the Autopilot and auto throttle; removed his hand from the speed brake and placed his hand on the thrust lever. He then called for flaps 20; then 25. As the plane decelerated to target speed; I heard the stick shaker and immediately retracted the speed brake and the stick shaker deactivated. We were stable by 1000 ft. and the remainder of the approach and landing was normal.I did see the FO remove his hand from the speed brake lever when he turned off the auto throttle. I should have said something to him; but speed brake extension did seem appropriate given the circumstance. While in the descent; the plane was slow to decelerate; and I wanted to make sure I did not extend flaps 25 within 10 kts. of the flaps 25 speed limit; and gave a little more attention to avoiding the possibility of a flap over speed instead of retracting the speed brakes before extending flaps 25. Some possible solutions to this problem would include vectors for another approach; or retracting the speed brake when the FO removed his hand.'
rationale: While managing a high-on-profile visual approach, the FO removed his hand from the speed brake lever to manage the auto throttle disconnect, leaving the speed brakes extended through the flap 25 selection—and the Captain observed this but chose not to intervene, directing his attention instead to monitoring flap-limit speeds. The competing attentional demands of managing the deceleration profile, monitoring flap speeds, and coordinating with the FO caused both crewmembers to allow a hazardous configuration to persist until the stick shaker activated. The approach procedures and speed/flap constraints were correct; the incident arose from inadequate crew resource management and divided attention allowing a critical configuration oversight to go uncorrected.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On final to land at ZZZ; my left landing gear struck approach landing light. We landed safely without injuries. Damage to prop/engine; left landing gear and left fuel tank.'
rationale: The pilot's left landing gear struck an approach lighting fixture during final approach, indicating the aircraft descended below the safe approach path sufficiently to contact ground-level infrastructure. The very brief narrative contains no mechanical or procedural anomaly—the aircraft was simply flown too low. This is a situational awareness failure in which the pilot did not maintain an accurate perception of the aircraft's position relative to the approach path and obstacle clearance, resulting in a low approach that contacted the lighting system.

text: Narrative 1 - 'My aircraft was cleared for a departure from LAS with a climbing right turn to a northerly heading and 3;000 feet. As I was reaching an altitude of 3;000 feet and a heading of roughly north; another aircraft made a call to tower saying that we had nearly collided. At this time I looked over my right shoulder and spotted another helicopter at my altitude approximately 200 feet behind me and 100 feet to the east. As I was making my climbing right turn I believe that aircraft was making a left turn above me and to my left rear at 3;000 feet. Luckily the pilot of the other aircraft was able to see me before we collided.'
rationale: Two helicopters operating at Las Vegas were issued clearances that placed them at the same altitude and in conflicting turn directions at 3,000 feet, resulting in a near-collision with approximately 200 feet lateral and zero vertical separation. The reporting pilot was executing a cleared climbing right turn while the other helicopter appeared to be turning toward him at the same altitude—a configuration that should have been prevented by ATC ensuring vertical or lateral separation before issuing the second clearance. The procedure error lies with the ATC clearance issuance: both aircraft were permitted to converge at 3,000 feet without an intervening separation standard, making this a failure of the clearance delivery procedure rather than any pilot performance lapse.

text: Narrative 1 - 'My instructor and I had gone flown out to ZZZ airport and back to Butler for my instrument training. Two other planes from the flight school were doing the same approaches as us and were essentially following with ATC providing separation. On our way back into BTP; we used the ILS-8 approach and broke off at standard pattern altitude to join the Downwind leg for Runway 26; which was the runway in use. Aircraft X joined the Downwind leg behind us with a standard 45 degree entry. At one point someone asked him where he was at to keep enough space between them and he reported his position abeam the numbers; which was where we were to prevent a collision we reported and executed an early base turn to a short approach to land. When one of the other planes in the pattern explained he was right on top of us he said; 'I'm in the downwind leg. I don't see anyone.' I feel like practicing good scanning and better spatial awareness could have prevented this.'
rationale: Aircraft X joined the traffic pattern downwind without visually acquiring the reporter's aircraft already established in the same position, then reported abeam the numbers—exactly where the reporter was—and when advised of the conflict, responded that he saw no one in the pattern. The Aircraft X crew's failure to scan for and acquire pattern traffic before and during their downwind entry demonstrates a breakdown in the see-and-avoid principle in an uncontrolled airport environment. All self-separation procedures were available and understood; the incident was caused by the conflicting pilot's inadequate visual scan and situational awareness in the pattern.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'I reached out to the First Officer a week prior to the trip and we had a brief discussion to schedule a meeting in order to discuss the upcoming IOE training. We met at my hangar and I went over a lot of the items on the OE/Requal checklist. I felt it was important to get some work done ahead of time as the [early morning] departure to ZZZ is a very difficult start time to manage with any kind of rest. I made a point to brief the areas of vulnerabilities for both ZZZ1 and ZZZ. These are arguably two of the more challenging airports we fly to; primarily because of the high field elevation. I debated for a long time whether I should fly both legs. I ultimately decided there is was no way I would let a new pilot with zero heavy jet experience land in ZZZ1. The weather in ZZZ was good; clear with no wind; so I felt this would be the acceptable option to begin the training.  We met one hour prior to departure and went over the flight paperwork and expectations for the flight. Arriving at the aircraft; preflight; and engine start were normal. Taxi out and takeoff as well as cruise phase and the descent into the ZZZ area were all fine. As part of the landing briefing; I made a point to cover the difference landing at a high elevation airport over a normal elevation airport. I told the First Officer it was important to flare at 30 feet instead of the normal 20 feet. We discussed the auto throttles and whether to leave them engaged or not. I told him I preferred to leave them engaged; but not to let them come to idle at 30 feet. I emphasized he could still push the throttles with the auto throttles engaged. I told him we should leave some power in until well established in the flare or when the wheels touch down. We were cleared for the visual approach and descended to 7000 feet. We had some difficulty intercepting the final approach course as we rolled out too early and were approximately a half mile left of the course. Now we were going past the glide slope intercept point and were getting slightly high on the path. I instructed the First Officer to disengage the autopilot and manually fly the aircraft. We were able to make all necessary flight path corrections and meet stabilized approach well prior to 1000 feet. The First Officer began his flare at 50 feet. This was slightly higher than the 30 feet we briefed. I told him to add power and he complied. I felt a sink rate develop around 20 feet and I exerted slight aft pressure on the yoke to cushion the touchdown. We were exactly on the approach speed of 135 knots; so I felt we had plenty of energy to work with. I think he felt the pressure I put on the yoke and he abruptly snatched the yoke aft just as the wheels touched down. The aircraft rapidly pitched up as the aircraft bounced. I felt the bounce was serious and immediately initiated a go around. I saw he was moving to deploy the reversers and I pushed his hands away and made sure the reversers remained stowed while I pushed the throttles to Max and hit the TOGA switches. The go around; pattern and subsequent landing were all normal. I filled out the logbook and made the appropriate phone calls. I felt we were in an extremely dangerous situation and recovering the landing was not an option.'
Narrative 2 - 'During the first leg of my OE; I was the flying pilot on the visual approach to RWY XXR at ZZZ backed up with the ILS. I was hand flying the approach with autothrottles engaged.  The approach was stabilized and at approximately 200 ft AGL I added a small amount of thrust to maintain speed given the high airport elevation. Between 50 ft and 30 ft I began to initiate a flare and at some point; shortly thereafter; the Captain added back pressure on the control wheel to counter what he felt was an increase in sink rate. The wheels touched down firmly and there was a perceived bounce. The Captain immediately took over the aircraft controls and initiated a go-around to avoid a second touchdown. The Captain flew the go-around and traffic pattern to a normal landing and we taxied to the ramp with no further issues. After a maintenance inspection; it was determined the aircraft did not suffer a hard landing or any damage; but the tail skid plate had contacted the runway. This was my first approach and landing in the actual aircraft and I believe the high airport elevation was a factor in aircraft control.'
rationale: The IOE FO began his flare 20 feet higher than briefed, and when the Captain applied back pressure to counter a developing sink rate, the FO simultaneously snatched the yoke aft at touchdown, causing a rapid pitch-up and serious bounce. The hazardous outcome resulted from two distinct human performance failures: the FO's imprecise flare initiation at an unfamiliar high-elevation airport on his first actual aircraft approach, and an uncoordinated dual-input in the flare that amplified the pitch response. The landing technique and high-elevation briefing procedures were correct and had been explicitly reviewed; the failure was in the FO's execution under pressure and the breakdown of stick-input coordination at the critical moment.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was experiencing intermittent low amp readings; fluctuating from 60 to -5 amps. I was analyzing this situation perhaps more aggressively than necessary which led to a failure to switch fuel tanks. I had more than an hour of reserve fuel on board and was monitoring and managing my fuel. When reaching 8 gallons remaining on my left rank I made a mental note to switch at 5 gallons. However; I turned my attention back to my low volts indication and completely forgot about switching fuel tanks. Bottom line; the engine stopped; I [advised ATC] and landed without incident at ZZZ. Once on the ground; it finally occurred to me that I forgot to switch fuel tanks.'
rationale: The pilot was preoccupied monitoring and analyzing an intermittent low-amperage electrical indication, a secondary fault that drew attention away from the primary fuel management task. Although the pilot had made a mental note to switch fuel tanks at 5 gallons remaining, the distraction of the electrical anomaly caused complete loss of that intention, and the engine stopped from fuel exhaustion on the active tank. This is a textbook distraction-induced prospective memory failure: the correct fuel management procedure was known and planned; the human operator's cognitive focus on an unrelated fault caused the scheduled task to be forgotten entirely.

text: Narrative 1 - 'PA28 took off out of ZZZ RWY XX while student and I were approaching from the west on 45º to the left downwind for RWY XX. We made radio calls at 8 and 3 mi from the airport. We heard the Archer make his takeoff call while we were around 5 mi from the airport. Shortly after we made our 3 mi call. Almost immediately after the PA28 made a call along the lines of '3 mi northwest of the airport turning south' I don't recall any mention of altitude in the call. Almost immediately after the G1000 Traffic alert goes off; the student calls traffic in sight; and realizing the proximity I immediately started a climb. At start of climb we were probably only 300 above the traffic; when they passed under us they could not have been more than 500 below.'
rationale: A PA28 on departure climbed to pattern altitude and converged with the instructor's aircraft on approach without the departing pilot acquiring or maintaining awareness of the inbound traffic, ultimately closing to within 300 feet vertically before the G1000 traffic alert prompted the instructor's evasive climb. The PA28's radio call omitted altitude information, reducing the positional awareness available to other traffic, and the pilot apparently did not scan for or detect the inbound aircraft on the 45-degree entry to the downwind. The conflict arose from the departing pilot's failure of situational awareness and incomplete radio reporting, not from any procedural ambiguity or ATC failure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On the ILSXXR approach to ZZZ; in between ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1; I accidentally descended well below the posted IFR altitude of 3100 MSL to roughly 2700 MSL before realizing and correcting. I was performing this approach autopilot coupled; and I missed that the autopilot had not captured the glide slope properly and was descending below my intended altitude. I realized this before ZZZ Tower called out the low altitude; and then they did; I advised I was correcting. I was on a filed IFR flight plan to ZZZ; but conditions were VFR at the time.'
rationale: While flying an autopilot-coupled ILS approach, the pilot did not monitor the flight director and flight mode annunciator closely enough to detect that the autopilot had failed to capture the glideslope, allowing the aircraft to descend approximately 400 feet below the published IFR minimum segment altitude before the error was self-detected and corrected. The ILS procedure and autopilot system were functioning as designed; the failure was the pilot's inadequate monitoring of the automation state—a human-machine interface and situational awareness lapse that allowed an undetected autopilot mode deviation to persist through a significant altitude excursion.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Forgot to arm thrust reversers on landing. Was very distracted. We got vector super high onto the approach. My FO was getting the glideslope back for a stable approach and once everything was good my FO recalls right when we called for the landing checklist Tower asked for bases. Just got very distracted. Also; this is my first trip after 85 days off.We got triggers and checklist. Just need to follow them and learn from this silly mistake!'
rationale: The crew omitted the thrust reverser arming step on landing after a high-workload approach sequence: an unexpected high vector requiring rapid reconfiguration, an ATC frequency request interrupting the landing checklist, and the Captain's first trip after an 85-day absence from operations. The Captain himself identified distraction and lack of currency as the root causes. The checklist procedure and arming triggers were correct and in place; the failure was the human operator's checklist execution breaking down under the combined pressures of task overload, interruption at a critical phase, and diminished procedural currency from a prolonged absence.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Improperly secured Dangerous Goods (DG). Again a box containing DG was sent to the aircraft for loading with the DG only secured with one strap.'
rationale: Ground personnel sent a dangerous goods shipment to the aircraft secured with only a single strap rather than the required securing standard, and the reporter notes this is a recurring occurrence. The DG securing requirements were established and known; the individual responsible for preparing and tendering the shipment failed to apply them correctly. This is a human factors error of non-compliance with documented handling procedures by the ground personnel involved, rather than any ambiguity or gap in the written DG securing requirements themselves.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I have asked my Relief Pilot to inspect the main deck after loading was completed. He brought to my attention stacking issues with pallet position RL. I have asked ZZZ Load Master to address his concerns and delayed departure until the issues was fixed. After landing ZZZ1; ground personnel offloaded position RL and RR and we learned that pallet position SR collapsed into pallet position SL. Both S position were hazardous lithium batteries.'
rationale: During cargo loading, the Relief Pilot identified and flagged stacking issues with pallet RL, prompting corrective action before departure, but the adjacent SR and SL pallet positions—both loaded with hazardous lithium batteries—were not identified as unstable and subsequently collapsed in-flight. The human factor is the incomplete pre-departure cargo inspection: while one loading problem was caught, the inspection failed to identify a second, adjacent instability in a hazmat-designated position, indicating an insufficient inspection of the full cargo deck before departure.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'The aircraft in question was operating with MEL 27-XXX open; Spoilers #2 or #4. After engine start on both legs; an ECAM message was received regarding the spoilers but this was disregarded as a result of the MEL. Upon further reflection; these messages should have triggered further investigation on my part; as the presence of such an alert was not mentioned in the MEL. However; I had noted a write-up in the MEL about spurious messages - made by a Check Airman - which was subsequently signed off by Maintenance as essentially being unimportant. The aircraft had flown 4 legs the day prior with the MEL active. Also; at the time of this incident; I had approximately 100 hours of experience in any Airbus aircraft.The first leg of the day was flown uneventfully. Immediately after takeoff on our second leg of the day with the same plane; an ECAM caution and audio alert was triggered for a F/CTL SPLR FAULT. The message subsequently cleared itself; but then a few seconds later; would trigger again. This cycle repeated itself approximately every 20-30 seconds for about 20 minutes; until the flight reached FL270; where the message stopped cycling and instead remained. Initially; after exiting the sterile period; ACARS messages were sent and a brief exchange ensued. An emergency return to ZZZ was offered as an option; but given that we were well above maximum landing weight and the ECAM did not require us to land as soon as possible; I elected to continue the climb.Upon reaching cruise altitude; I transferred the flying duties to the FO (First Officer) and verbally contacted Dispatch and eventually Maintenance Control. Maintenance had no inflight suggestions and stated that they would look at it when the plane is on the ground. At this point; the Dispatcher and I discussed our options. Since the ECAM mentioned FUEL CONSUMPTION INCREASED and FMS PRED UNRELIABLE and we still had just under 3 hours to go on a flight planned to land with just 8;000 LBS of fuel; the decision was made to divert to ZZZ1. Dispatch changed our destination and we proceeded to an uneventful landing in ZZZ1. An emergency was not declared. While on the ground in ZZZ1; one of the responding mechanics reported to me that he discovered that the MEL procedures were not properly complied with when the item was deferred and that Spoiler Pair #2 was inadvertently deactivated instead of Pair #4. Apparently; the aircraft had flown 4 legs in this condition prior to our operating it. He mentioned to me his concern that on the [day prior]; a previous crew; the previously mentioned Check Airman had also apparently experienced a similar condition; wrote it in the logbook; only to have Maintenance simply clear the entry by writing 'info noted.' He expected that the aircraft would be taken out of service to fix the underlying issue. However; the decision was made to clear the write-up that I made and allow the deferral to continue. Based on the information that was relayed to me by this Mechanic; it appears that the initial error of deactivating the wrong spoiler pair was attempting to be covered up. This is very concerning.My relative inexperience with Airbus aircraft allowed me to be lulled into a false sense of security when it came to ECAM messages that appeared to relate to the MEL item. Seeing a write-up about spurious messages being handled the way it had been also led me to believe that there was nothing out of the ordinary; when realistically; there was a major error made by a certificated Mechanic. In retrospect; what was displayed on the F/CTL page should have alerted me to a problem; with both Spoiler Pairs #2 and #4; but I assumed that this was the same display that the mechanics and all of the other pilots who operated the aircraft before me also saw. The MEL does not give us specific guidance as to what should be displayed on this page with the MEL active; but I presume that the AMM (Aircraft Maintenance Manual) procedure referenced should tell the Mechanic whatshould have been displayed.More information in the MEL document detailing what exactly should be displayed on the SD (System Display) and what messages the crew should or shouldn't expect for a given MEL. Also; if there was indeed an attempt to cover up a maintenance error; I think this case should be used as an example as to how to properly correct an error.'
Narrative 2 - 'After takeoff we got an ECAM for flight spoiler fault that was reoccurring. We had this aircraft on the previous leg and got the ECAM and cleared it but this one kept returning. We still thought it was associated with the MEL on the Aircraft Spoiler Surface 4 INOP but Spoiler Surface 2 was also INOP. The Captain had reviewed the logbook before the previous flight and seen another crew had this happen and Maintenance had signed off [that] it was normal for the MEL. The Captain got in touch [with] Maintenance through Dispatch and they wanted us to divert to ZZZ1. We diverted and landed uneventfully.I think Maintenance improperly deferred the Spoiler Surface 4 and after a crew wrote it up; did not verify it was correct. The MEL should have had us view the Flight Control page instead of the HYD SD page for amber information of #4 Spoiler ONLY amber or #2 Spoiler ONLY.'
rationale: Maintenance incorrectly deactivated Spoiler Pair #2 instead of the MEL-mandated Pair #4, flew the aircraft four legs in this misconfigured state, and when a previous crew wrote up the discrepancy, Maintenance cleared the logbook entry as 'info noted' without correcting the underlying error—a pattern that the reporting mechanic characterized as an attempted cover-up. The MEL document itself lacked specific guidance on what the Flight Control System Display should show with the deferral active, removing the crew's ability to independently verify correct MEL execution. The procedure error is compounded and systemic: initial incorrect MEL compliance, a deficient MEL that provided no crew-verifiable configuration standard, and a maintenance quality-control process that actively suppressed detection of the error rather than correcting it.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'Assigned the MIA Hedly 1 Departure from 8L.  Clearance was misunderstood to mean we were to fly the departure as depicted.  We reviewed the departure and the flight plan IAW the FOM and AOM procedures.  Upon reaching 8L we were cleared for takeoff.  I do not recall hearing an assigned heading nor were we told to 'RNAV to Senoy'; the first fix on the DP (Departure Plate).  We departed in Nav mode.After takeoff the FMS captured and steered us essentially straight ahead to Senoy.  At Senoy; we began a left turn toward the next fix Cimba.  At that point ATC queried and suggested that we may have taken someone else's heading.  The Second in Command (pilot not flying) replied that we were following the SID as cleared.  ATC said that was incorrect and gave us an amended heading; while I kept the aircraft slow until we sorted it out.Miami said they would talk to the tower to figure out what we were assigned; but we never learned the result of that call; if it was ever made.  The most telling comment from ATC was 'You wouldn't be the first one to do that; it's something we're working with the FAA on right now.'  That was the last thing we heard about it.It seems that there was some confusion on the part of the Second in Command when the clearance was received.  I was not present at that time; but I found it interesting that even following the FOM procedures for route verification; that initial copying of the clearance is a potential single point of failure.It seems that there is some known issue either with this departure or in how it is verbalized by Miami ATC. The controller's statement suggests that this is not an isolated incident; but it is currently being addressed.  I am not suggesting that we require both pilots to be present for the copying of the clearance. There are far too many duties placed on us already; and more show up by the day; including now passenger iPad maintenance; etc.There is simply too much required by our Company in getting a plane ready for flight to have yet another requirement for both pilots to be in the cockpit to receive a clearance.  Errors will happen; and I will be more cognizant to ask a question if I think something is out of the ordinary.  Adding another layer of redundancy in this case will likely lead to something else; perhaps outside of the aircraft; to be missed; unintended consequences.'
Narrative 2 - 'I called Miami clearance delivery to get our clearance from MIA.  According to our company filed flight plan; we were filed for 'MIA..HEDLY J53…' I copied and read back 'Aircraft X cleared via radar vectors HEDLY as filed; maintain 5000 expect 400 10 min. after departure. Departure on 119.45; squawk XXXX'. The controller on clearance delivery corrected me by saying 'Vectors to HEDLY1 RNAV departure.'  With his emphasis on the HEDLY1 RNAV departure; which actually ends at HEDLY; I misunderstood the clearance to think that we were to takeoff and fly to SENOY and then follow the HEDLY1 RNAV departure. Upon reviewing our clearance with the captain prior to start; I was so certain that we were to fly the RNAV; that the captain didn't have any reason to question the clearance. We took off on runway 08L and flew out to SENOY where we commenced a left turn towards CIMBA on the RNAV departure.  Shortly after that turn; our departure controller said we must have taken someone else's turn by mistake.  I said we were just flying the RNAV departure.  We were given a tight turn to west.  I apologized for my mistake and we received a comment from the controller that we were not the first ones to have made that mistake and that we're working with the FAA right now to fix that.  Very shortly after that we were cleared direct to HEDLY and as filed.I was very mistaken to think that a vector meant to fly the published departure; but I was confused by the correction to my read back that it was the HEDLY1 RNAV departure not just vectors to HEDLY.  While it is not required; the tower controller who cleared us for takeoff might have said 'Fly runway heading' or assigned us a heading for vectors; though I am not certain that it would have alerted me to the error in my thinking of the RNAV departure.On our next leg flying out of Airport Y; we were waiting for 6 to 8 other departures prior to ours.  Airport Y was using the RNAV departure; and every single aircraft departing on RWY XY was 'Cleared for takeoff runway XY; RNAV to [fix on SID]'. That left no doubt as to what that crew was expected to do.'
rationale: Miami ATC's correction of the FO's readback—'Vectors to HEDLY1 RNAV departure'—was ambiguous enough that the FO interpreted it as a clearance to fly the full published SID routing rather than vectors to the HEDLY fix, and the crew then executed accordingly. The tower controller's takeoff clearance included no runway heading or explicit vector instruction that would have cued the crew to their error before the conflict developed. Most significantly, the ATC controller who detected the deviation acknowledged 'you wouldn't be the first one to do that—it's something we're working with the FAA on right now,' confirming this clearance format was a known, recurring source of pilot misinterpretation at this facility.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X was switched to me by EWR dept level at 060. I climbed him but before he began his climb the conflict alert went off because Aircraft Y turned eastbound climbing right into Aircraft X who was at 060. I issued traffic and continued Aircraft X climb.The EWR area should have made a point out of Aircraft Y to me via landline and making an visual point out (yellow) on my radar scope.'
Narrative 2 - 'Aircraft Y was a slow climbing small aircraft off of LDJ airport whose first fix was Brezy. He was given multiple vectors in order to climb him inside my airspace but was left on a heading of 090 for too long when climbing out of 050. Aircraft X was a EWR departure going out the east gate to LGA departure on a 040 heading and 060 altitude. I don't know if there was a late switch on the pilot's part or if the LGA controller decided not to climb or turn the a/c once he had control of him like they usually do. I did not expect the a/c to still be at level flight on the heading I shipped him on. I saw the converging targets between the two a/c and had Aircraft Y immediately stop his climb and descend back down to 050. He was at 054 when given the descent and traffic and showed 055 before he started his descent. I gave Aircraft Y a turn northbound initially before i realized the conflict; he continued in the turn while descending. I expected Aircraft X to be climbed and turned like he typically is by the LGA controller and had Aircraft Y climbing in the path I shipped him on. No procedure change; I had expectation bias and messed up. I don't know if it was because of time on position; working new surroundings; or any other new thing since moving from New York to Philadelphia facility.'
rationale: The EWR departure area switched Aircraft Y—a slow-climbing small aircraft on a heading of 090—to the adjacent sector without performing the required landline point-out or placing a yellow visual point-out on the receiving controller's scope, leaving the receiving controller without the coordination information needed to anticipate the conflict with Aircraft X climbing on a 040 heading at 060. The receiving controller, not having been informed of Aircraft Y's routing and climb, developed expectation bias about that aircraft's behavior, but the structural cause was the upstream sector's failure to execute the mandatory inter-facility point-out procedure before the handoff. Had the required coordination been completed, the receiving controller would have had the situational picture needed to prevent the conflict alert.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On an early evening flight to IAH we were descending via the DRLLR 5 RNAV arrival being set up and briefed for the RNAV Y 26R with our altitude set to 6000'. When checking on with the approach controller we were told to expect the runway 26L transition. We selected the new runway in the FMS but failed to reset our altitude selector to 7;000' at that time. As we neared ZOEEE Drag Required appeared on the FMS scratchpad and the Captain said; 'Careful not to be too hot at ZOEEE'. I deployed the speed brakes and slowed us to 210kts and checked the FMA and we were still in VNAV PATH. Shortly after passing ZOEEE; I noticed the FMA showed VNAV SPEED as we were leveling at 6;000' and I no longer showed SKLER as a waypoint on the screen. I then looked more closely at the DRLLR 5 chart and noticed the point we'd briefed as the lowest altitude on the arrival had (Runway 26R) beside it and thought we should've stopped at 7;000'. I brought it to the attention of the captain who then queried ATC. The ATC response was that we were ok and to now descend to 5;000'. We started the descent and shortly after were told by what sounded like a different voice on the radio to maintain 6;000'. We reversed the approximately 200' descent and returned to 6000'.The rest of the approach and landing were uneventful and the flight ended safely at the destination. I believe we should've stopped at 7;000' but am unsure since the controller didn't have a problem with us being at 6;000' so I listed the event as a possible altitude deviation.'
rationale: After being assigned a late transition change from runway 26R to 26L on the DRLLR 5 arrival, the crew updated the runway selection in the FMS but failed to simultaneously reset the altitude selector from 6,000 to the 26L transition's 7,000-foot constraint, resulting in the aircraft leveling at the incorrect altitude. The crew self-detected the discrepancy when reviewing the approach chart, and ATC confirmed no conflict existed. The error was a workload-driven omission during a late-stage reconfiguration: updating one element of the approach setup (runway selection) while neglecting the linked altitude update is a classic incomplete task execution under time pressure, a human factors failure rather than any ambiguity in the arrival procedure itself.

text: Narrative 1 - 'At XA:00; I was scheduled to take a local student solo from ZZZ [airport] to build solo flight time. Before the flight; I checked the aviation weather government website as part of my standard before-flight weather briefing. ZZZ showed winds 8kts at 180 with no gusts. However; airports in the vicinity; most notably ZZZ1 [airport]; were showing gusts at 18kts.The first mistake was electing to take a solo in varying wind conditions. This was an unsafe choice and showed poor ADM (Aeronautical Decision Making). At XA:10; I had pre-flighted the aircraft but had not confirmed that I was absolutely clear about the weather from my CFI. There was an exchange of text messages; but I was never officially cleared to fly.This is the second mistake; I should have made absolutely certain that my CFI had cleared me to fly in the weather that was presented.Takeoff was at XA:20; and the flight was normal. Turbulence was present but expected; and all maneuvers were executed at a level that I was comfortable with. At XB:25; I began the short return to ZZZ from the state practice area. I listened to the ATIS and was surprised to hear the winds were now variable at 10kts; gusting at 18kts.At this moment; I should have contacted my CFI either through my phone or by contacting the tower. I am not rated or endorsed to fly in crosswinds stronger than 8kts; so I was operating outside the FARs that governed my flight limitations. I decided to press on with landing; knowing that it could be a dangerous situation but not considering my alternative actions.While at the time; I considered landing at ZZZ my only reasonable choice; looking back; it is clear that I had many different options. I had a full tank of fuel and could have waited for an updated ATIS. While the winds were a crosswind at ZZZ; there are many airports around ZZZ that may have had a headwind-bearing runway. Because I was operating outside the FAR; I should have [advised ATC] to alert the airport to my situation.I entered the base leg for runway XXL as ATC instructed and turned to final; I was at flaps 30 and 65kts.I have been trained and unfortunately did not apply the lesson that I should have used as few flaps as possible for crosswind landings and come in slightly faster at 70kts.Ten feet above the runway; I was struck by the first gust of wind; which lifted my aircraft slightly and then pushed it back down.At this point; I should have commenced a go-around; but I was target fixated on landing the aircraft and not on piloting the aircraft.On touchdown; my right wheel grabbed the runway; but no other wheel did. A second gust of wind came from my left and lifted/turned the aircraft right. I remember putting a full left rudder; but I am unsure of my aileron attitude. Though I believe it was also left.I should have input the correct crosswind technique of using the aileron to keep to the centerline and the rudder to keep the nose straight.At this point; going around was no longer an option. I was more than 30 degrees off-center and was entering the grass. Inputting full throttle probably would have ended with a crash into the hangers. However; I was able to ensure that neither wing nor prop struck the runway or grass; and I applied aerodynamic braking to slow the plane; finally coming to rest a few feet before taxiway 1.As is said; few accidents or incidents occur simply from one mistake. It is a compounding series of errors that led to my runway excursion; and looking back at what had happened; the simplest and easiest choice would have been not to go flying; at least not solo. I am happy that I am uninjured; and while there is no official word on the aircraft; the post-flight showed no damage.This has been an unrivaled learning experience in my flight education so far. It reminds me that no one is safe from the weather and that small choices and errors can lead to much larger and far more devastating consequences later in the flight. I had to succumb to the'I need to complete this flight' or 'pressing ahead' mentality that has so often hurt or killed aviators. This was the last solo flight I needed to complete my solo requirements; and I didn't want to have to delay it after it had been delayed three times prior. The weather was marginal winds when I left; and 'flying by the seat of my pants' has proven in one single stroke to be a terrible choice. The best choice is and always will be to stay on the ground.'
rationale: The student pilot made a cascading sequence of aeronautical decision-making failures: departing solo without explicit CFI clearance in marginal crosswind conditions, continuing the approach after winds increased beyond their 8-knot crosswind endorsement limit, committing to land instead of going around when a gust lifted the aircraft, and applying incorrect crosswind technique at touchdown—ultimately exiting the runway. The student explicitly identifies each poor decision and the 'get-there-itis' mentality that sustained them. The applicable regulations, endorsement limits, and crosswind technique were all known; the incident resulted entirely from repeated human judgment failures in aeronautical decision-making.

text: Narrative 1 - 'We were cleared for the ZEEKK1 arrival expecting the ILS 26R approach into IAH.  This arrival has strict airspeed and altitude constraints at different waypoints along the procedure.  Because of the dissimilar aircraft on the arrival ([heavy] and smaller aircraft) ATC had us and the other aircraft fly airspeeds different than those published; everyone had a different speed. This created a lot of extra communications on a very crowded frequency.  Then ATC switched runways for us to 26L and tells us to resume published speeds as per the procedure.  It's very busy now as we set up for the different runway; manage the speeds and get a frequency change.  Once checked in; this controller says to expect 26R!  We told him the previous controller said 26L. He states 'I don't know why he said that but I need you on the right'. So again we switched everything back to 26R.  Very quickly he clears us to ZAPPO (the IAF for 26R) maintain 6;000 and intercept the LOC.  The read back did not include 'maintain 6;000' ATC didn't catch it; nor did I.  We were task saturated at this point as we switched RWY's again; trying honor the arrival constraints and follow ATC instructions. ZAPPO is 5000A so I put 5;000 in the MCP window and then started the descent. Just prior to the fix; the controller stated' I didn't clear you to 5;000; told you to maintain 6;000; It's OK there's no conflict; cleared the approach contact tower.'  The approach and landing were uneventful; no further communications from ATC about this.'
rationale: ATC issued two consecutive runway changes during an active STAR arrival—first switching from 26R to 26L, then reversing back to 26R—each requiring the crew to reconfigure the FMS, re-brief the approach, manage new speed constraints, and handle frequency changes in a compressed timeframe. This ATC-generated task saturation directly caused the crew to miss the 'maintain 6,000' restriction during the second switch's readback, with neither pilot nor controller catching the omission. The crew's altitude deviation was a downstream consequence of an ATC operational procedure that imposed two successive, time-critical runway changes on a crew already managing the demands of a speed-constrained arrival—the procedural breakdown originated with ATC's traffic management sequencing, not with any crew error in normal operations.

text: Narrative 1 - 'During a repositioning flight departing from ZZZ climbing through 15;000 ft.; the First Officer and I noticed that the cabin altitude displayed 8;800 ft. in amber and was climbing slowly. We were cleared to 17;000 ft.; and upon reaching; the cabin aural warning had just gone off and the displayed altitude of 10;500 ft. was in red. Upon noticing this; we coordinated with ATC to descend to 15;000 ft. in order to help with the pressurization. Reaching 15;000 ft.; we ran the QRH and determined that the manual controller knob was not in the auto position (full left); but was closer to the dump position. After correcting the knob's position back to auto; the plane began to re-pressurize. We stayed at 15;000 ft. for a few minutes to assist with the pressurization and once it achieved safe levels; we began a slow climb to our final altitude of 21;000 ft.. We then continued the flight with constant monitoring of the system and no further issues.The issue was detected by the flight crew during climb out and noticing that the cabin altitude on the EICAS was abnormal.The cause of this event was due both pilots missing items at certain points. During the FO's final cockpit scan; the landing field elevation was selected but the knob's position was not fully verified. Also; during climb out and the after takeoff checklist; the Pilot Monitoring didn't effectively check the pressurization.The Flight Crew reacted appropriately to the issue by leveling off to troubleshoot; descending to help with the pressurization; and running the QRH and discovering the problem. We also continued to monitor the situation for the remainder of the flight.There are certainly a few things to suggest here. First; it is vitally important that both crew members thoroughly review everything all the time; especially with a plane that has just come out of maintenance. Second; it is equally important to make sure checklists are run to their completion - meaning that the expanded portions of the checklists are also adhered to. Lastly; I would suggest that; if possible; Maintenance goes over the actions taken and switches moved with the Flight Crew before a repo flight leaves any station. While it is the responsibility of the crew to conduct a safe and compliant flight; it is often assumed that maintenance has done their best to leave the plane as a crew would receive it on the line. Sometimes there are switches and lights out of place; but I have never experienced an item as important as pressurization until now. I will continue to be exceptionally vigilant in the future.'
rationale: After receiving the aircraft from maintenance, both the FO's pre-departure cockpit scan and the Pilot Monitoring's after-takeoff checklist failed to detect that the pressurization manual controller knob had been left out of the auto position, allowing the cabin altitude to climb to 10,500 feet before being caught and corrected. The crew identified the root cause themselves: the FO verified the landing field elevation selection but did not fully confirm the knob position, and the PM's after-takeoff checklist review was insufficiently thorough. The pressurization system and checklist procedures were correct; both crewmembers' incomplete execution of standard scan and checklist verification steps was the human factors failure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'On arrival passing through 10;000 ft. we encountered some kind of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) that crossed above us head on with very narrow separation. ATC had no other aircraft on their scope. The vehicle had no wings or rotors and had a blinking light in the nose such as a strobe. This occurred just about 4 miles south of the CAPKO fix on the RAVEN6 arrival. No evasive action was taken and the flight continued uneventfully.'
rationale: On descent through 10,000 feet, the crew encountered an unlit UAV crossing head-on with very narrow vertical separation that did not appear on ATC radar—indicating the UAV operator was flying well into controlled airspace at altitude without a transponder or any form of ATC coordination. The flight crew had no prior warning and no time to take evasive action; their response was appropriate given the zero reaction time available. The incident is classified as human factors because the hazard was entirely created by an external operator's failure of situational awareness and regulatory compliance in operating an untracked UAV in active controlled airspace.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Much like several of my previous reports Aircraft X was entering the sector non-radar without 3 different controllers recognizing it would potentially conflict with traffic along its route. We got lucky yet again and there happened to be no traffic. This was a particularly hard to catch routing as his first strip posting was a fix that is a very uncommon posting for non-radar traffic.I amend my suggestion on how to fix this. A better plan than all the fixes on the boundary is to send the Controller a update that the next sector has accepted coordination on Aircraft X just as it does outbound. This would make the situation very hard to occur. We have discussed this solution at the facility with good responses. I was hoping that you could help generate momentum at the facility leadership level to get this done ASAP.'
rationale: The reporter documents a recurring pattern—across multiple previous ASRS reports—in which non-radar aircraft enter sectors without the receiving controllers recognizing the potential route conflicts, relying on luck to avoid incidents each time. The root cause is a systemic gap in the non-radar coordination toolset: no automated notification exists to confirm to the receiving controller that an adjacent sector has accepted coordination on an incoming non-radar aircraft, as exists for outbound coordination. Because the deficiency is structural—the same gap causes the same failure repeatedly across multiple controllers and multiple events—it is a procedure and systems design failure, not the result of any individual's performance lapse.
